Efficiency enhancement of energy supply chains using a machine 
learning-driven network evaluation framework for blockchain adoption
Ardavan Babaei a,b,*, Erfan Babaee Tirkolaee b,c,d,***
, Shahryar Sorooshian e,**
,  
Sadia Samar Ali f, Gongming Wang g
a Faculty of Industrial Engineering, K. N. Toosi University of Technology, Tehran, Iran
b Department of Industrial Engineering, Istinye University, Istanbul, Türkiye
c Department of Industrial Engineering and Management, Yuan Ze University, Taoyuan, Taiwan
d Department of Mechanics and Mathematics, Western Caspian University, Baku, Azerbaijan
e Departement of Business Administration, University of Gothenburg, Gothenburg, Sweden
f Department of Industrial Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia
g Faculty of Information Technology, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing, China
A R T I C L E  I N F O
Handling Editor: Dr. Mark Howells
Keywords:
Blockchain adoption
Decision-making
Network data envelopment analysis
Energy industry
Oil and gas supply chain
A B S T R A C T
The Oil and Gas (O&G) sector is essential to the global energy supply, yet it still struggles with inefficiencies 
caused by conventional, paper-driven decision-making processes in its supply chain. This study aims to assess the 
integration of blockchain technology as a strategic solution to these challenges. It examines strategies for 
blockchain integration considering organizational, supply chain, technological, and external factors. A novel 
approach based on four network-based data-driven optimization models is utilized to investigate a case study in 
Norway’s O&G sector over a five-year period (April to October 2020). These models incorporate uncertainty to 
assess the effectiveness of various blockchain integration strategies. The findings demonstrate that “single-use 
strategy” is effective in low-risk scenarios, while “substitution strategy” is optimal in high-risk scenarios. The 
application of the Super-Efficiency Network Data Envelopment Analysis (SENDEA) model represents its 
robustness in evaluating blockchain adoption within the industry. Furthermore, a an efficient Machine Learning 
(ML) technique; i.e., Principal Component Analysis (PCA), is utilized to further assess blockchain adoption 
strategies. Therefore, policymakers are recommended to support risk-based blockchain adoption by directing 
organizations to implement single-use strategies in low-risk environments and substitution strategies in high-risk 
circumstances. Additionally, they can take advantage of practical evaluation frameworks provided by the 
SENDEA and uncertain models to systematically appraise and prioritize blockchain strategies based on their 
potential impacts.
1. Introduction
Affordable and sustainable energy serves as the basis for develop­
ment in key areas such as agriculture, education, healthcare, and 
transportation [1]. Although progress toward global energy goals has 
been consistent, it remains insufficient. Overcoming this challenge re­
quires significant investment in renewable energy infrastructure; e.g., 
solar, wind, and thermal systems, integrated with advancements in 
technology and grid modernization, particularly in developing 
countries. Achieving Sustainable Development Goal 7 (SDG 7: guaran­
teeing affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy access for 
everyone) requires countries to accelerate electrification, increase in­
vestment in renewable energy, enhance energy efficiency standards, and 
implement supportive policies. The involvement of the private sector 
plays a crucial role, with enterprises and investors promoting sustain­
able innovations and practices. Deploying blockchain solutions can 
facilitate faster achievement of SDG 7 b y improving transparency, 
operational efficiency, and confidence in the energy industry. For

The 27th United Nations Climate Change Conference (COP27) was 
held in Sharm El-Sheikh, Egypt, from 6 to November 20, 2022 under the 
theme “Together for Implementation” and chaired by Egypt on behalf of 
the African region [3]. Almost 35,000 delegates from 198 countries 
convened to fast-track action under the Paris Agreement. While COP26 
focused on setting targets, COP27 prioritized implementation, although 
advances in emissions reduction were still insufficient. Despite urging 
for revised emissions targets, only 29 nations put forward stronger Na­
tionally Determined Contributions (NDCs), with global temperatures 
still forecasted to rise by 2.4 ◦C. UN Secretary-General Ant´onio Guterres 
has called on global leaders to take meaningful action, cautioning that 
present efforts are far from meeting the 1.5 ◦C target. Blockchain can 
support the implementation focus of COP27 by enhancing transparency, 
accountability, and traceability in climate actions.
Behera et al. [4] examined the challenge of balancing economic 
growth and ecological sustainability in BRICS nations (i.e., Brazil, 
Russia, India, China, and South Africa). They demonstrated that while 
hydro and nuclear energy use, along with political stability, increases 
the ecological footprint, green technology innovation significantly re­
duces it. Moreover, they recommended coordinated policies to enhance 
regulatory frameworks, promote technological innovation, and facilitate 
clean energy adoption for sustainable development. In another study, 
Behera et al. [5] analyzed the impact of renewable (hydro and nuclear) 
and non-renewable (coal and oil) energy consumption on India’s eco­
nomic growth from 1985 to 2021. The findings showed that while hydro 
and oil consumption impede growth, nuclear and coal energy augment 
long-term economic growth. They recommended investing in renewable 
energy to balance India’s energy mix for sustainable growth. It was 
shown that blockchain can enhance transparency, accountability, and 
real-time monitoring of energy use, supporting clean energy adoption 
and policy coordination for sustainable growth in BRICS nations.
Investigating blockchain integration within O&G operations is 
essential to advancing SDG 7. This technology improves transparency 
across the supply chain, increases operational efficiency, and builds trust 
among stakeholders. The immutable data records of blockchain enable 
precise monitoring of emissions, aiding energy companies in aligning 
with the Paris Agreement and adhering to regulatory standards. In 
addition, by creating transparent and verifiable environmental records, 
blockchain can fundamentally change how decarbonization efforts are 
tracked and validated across the sector.
1.1. Blockchain technology and blockchain in the O&G sector
In the last decade, blockchain technology has become a pervasive 
trend in energy industries due to its vast benefits [6,7]. For example, in 
responding to the O&G sector’s digitalization, major companies explore 
blockchain’s potential benefits in management, efficiency, and data 
security [8]. According to Lu et al. [8], Europe and Asia spearhead 
blockchain development in O&G, yet operational projects are globally 
limited. Despite opportunities like reduced transaction costs and 
improved transparency, challenges endure, including industry under­
standing gaps, experimental applications, and inadequate investment. 
Blockchain transforms the data-intensive O&G industry by enhancing 
efficiency in administrative functions and addressing challenges like 
price volatility. Lakhanpal & Samuel [9] highlighted its benefits in the 
supply chain, regulatory compliance, database management, and 
cybersecurity and enabled industry stakeholders to better understand its 
potential to transform transparency, and enhance security and effi­
ciency. Existing systems in O&G Supply Chain Management (SCM) lack 
adequate transparency, security, and traceability. Our study investigates 
the capabilities of blockchain technology to offer decentralized, secure, 
and transparent solutions.
Ahmad et al. [10] comprehensively examined state-of-the-art 
blockchain schemes, initiatives, and case studies, illustrating its prac­
tical applicability in automating pivotal services, including product 
tracking, trade document protection, and the coordination of purchasing 
activities within the O&G industry. Blockchain technology, character­
ized by its decentralized, secure, and transparent architecture, has the 
potential to revolutionize sectors including finance, SCM, healthcare, 
and education. In the context of education, the integration of blockchain 
offers opportunities for enhancing efficiency, security, and credibility 
through the establishment of secure platforms facilitating the tracking of 
academic achievements [11]. It enhances the functionality of digital 
cryptocurrency, e.g., Bitcoins [12]. Blockchains are digital public led­
gers recording all Bitcoin transactions [13], visible to every member of 
the network, ensuring transparency. The structure makes data manip­
ulation difficult due to the hash values in each block, creating a secure 
environment. As blockchain technology demonstrates success in 
numerous cryptocurrencies, diverse organizations seek to utilize its 
transparency and fault tolerance to address challenges involving mul­
tiple untrusted actors in resource distribution.
1.2. Blockchain impact on SCM
Amiri et al. [14] proposed an innovative blockchain system design to 
enhance the supply chain of Engineering, Procurement, and Construc­
tion (EPC) companies in O&G infrastructure development, addressing 
cost and time inefficiencies. Their findings revealed significant re­
ductions in cost inefficiencies (12.4%) and operation lead times 
(36.5%). While limited by the confidentiality of the EPC company’s 
information, this research introduces a novel blockchain solution for 
project-development supply chains, contributing to improved efficiency 
and implementation processes. British Petroleum demonstrated that oil 
and natural gas collectively constitute 57% of the total energy 
consumed, with a projected expansion of 1.8% in global oil consumption 
and a 3% increase in natural gas consumption. Despite significant 
attention toward alternative energy sources, it is anticipated that O&G 
will maintain a share of over 50% in the global energy industry by 2040 
[15,16]. It is important for supply chains to maintain product quality 
during shipment. Traditional centralized management systems are 
vulnerable to corruption and misuse of authority. Blockchain provides a 
distributed solution, enhancing transparency and visibility [17]. The 
globalization of supply chains presents management challenges which 
can be efficiently treated by digitalization and improving resilience 
[18]. Blockchain technology, examined by Saberi et al. [19], is known as 
a potential to improve transparency, traceability, and security in global 
supply chains. They explored blockchain’s potential applications in SCM 
and investigated its role in supporting sustainability. They also identi­
fied potential adoption barriers, including inter-organizational, intra-­
organizational, technical, and external factors.
Each action in the supply chain, facilitated by these digital tech­
nologies, is systematically recorded on the blockchain [20]. This 
blockchain acts as an immutable repository for information universally 
accepted by all participating parties. Blockchain technology ensures 
confidentiality, integrity, and availability of transactions across a 
distributed ledger, revolutionizing various industries. Its flexible archi­
tecture accommodates a wide range of industry requirements, tackling 
key challenges related to usability, security, and cost. Built-in security 
features maintain the integrity and transparency of supply chain trans­
actions and underscore the technology’s transformative potential [21]. 
Numerous studies have emphasized blockchain’s potential to revolu­
tionize traditional supply chain practices, particularly in mitigating trust 
issues and improving visibility. With its shared ledger, blockchain im­
proves information transmission timeliness and diminishes costs asso­
ciated with searching and negotiating. Smart contracts further 
streamline operations, enhancing interactions among supply chain

3:
participants. Yet, despite its transformative potential, discussions on 
blockchain-based business models remain limited, with a critical need 
for comprehensive evaluation by supply chain practitioners [22]. 
Traceability plays a key role in tracking information and contributes 
significantly to supply chain transparency. As a decentralized ledger 
technology, blockchain resolves the limitations of centralized trace­
ability methods and ensures records are securely maintained. Inte­
grating it into supply chains augments transparency through the 
tracking of events.
1.3. Research objectives
Accordingly, the research objectives are also defined as follows. 
o Evaluating strategies for using blockchain specifically in the O&G 
industry,
o Introducing interrelated criteria for evaluating blockchain applica­
tion strategies based on supply chain, technology, drivers, and 
organization,
o Creating a network evaluation model to analyze the relationships 
between criteria in evaluating blockchain strategies,
o Employing Network Data Envelopment Analysis (NDEA) models to 
evaluate the effectiveness of blockchain deployment strategies,
o Integrating reality-based conditions, including uncertainty, into the 
evaluation models for a more comprehensive analysis of blockchain 
deployment strategies.
1.4. Research structure
This study introduces a novel methodology for evaluating blockchain 
implementation strategies in the O&G supply chain. Unlike previous 
work that has largely overlooked blockchain adoption in this sector, this 
research provides a comprehensive evaluation framework fine-tuned to 
align with the O&G industry’s priorities in terms of transparency, effi­
ciency, and resilience. Aligned with its objectives, to analyze blockchain 
strategies, establish interconnected evaluation criteria, and account for 
real-world uncertainties, the study combines data-driven and 
optimization-based techniques using NDEA. A key innovation is the 
development of models that not only reflect the complex relationships 
among evaluation factors but also assess risk under both stochastic and 
deterministic scenarios. We explore the interrelationships among eval­
uation criteria across various dimensions, including supply chain, 
technology, and organizational drivers. Principal Component Analysis 
(PCA) method is then utilized to transform optimal data into a simpler, 
more interpretable structure while preserving the most important pat­
terns of variation. As a result, it drives forward strategic decision- 
making in the adoption of blockchain technology, offering O&G sup­
ply chain managers a more accurate and practical tool for managing 
transformation in the energy sector.
The remaining sections are organized as follows. A literature survey 
is performed in Section 2 in order to highlight the gaps and contribu­
tions. Section 3 states the problem and describes the models. The ob­
tained results are presented in Section 4 which are then discussed in 
Section 5. Finally, Section 6 concludes the research and provides an 
outlook for future work.
2. Literature survey
2.1. Blockchain adoption in SCM
Xu et al. [23] investigated blockchain technology adoption in the 
automotive industry, highlighting its potential benefits for German 
OEMs, such as improved product information aggregation and supply 
chain reliability, while identifying obstacles such as technology imma­
turity and lack of standards. Waqar et al. [24] examined blockchain 
technology’s impact on construction supply chains, revealing its 
potential to enhance transparency, traceability, and efficiency through 
empirical case studies and industry surveys. Chiaraluce et al. [25] 
analyzed blockchain adoption in agri-food supply chains, particularly in 
wine and olive oil, highlighting its potential to enhance traceability, 
safety, and sustainability while addressing fragmented research. Amico 
and Cigolini [26] evaluated blockchain technology’s impact on port 
logistics, specifically the bill of lading lifecycle. Their simulation study 
revealed blockchain enhances trust, security, and efficiency, reducing 
container lead time by 3–4%. While the significance of employing 
blockchain in the supply chain, particularly within the O&G industry, is 
paramount, there is a paucity of literature offering optimization and 
data-driven models about its utilization in this domain. Babaei et al. [27] 
integrated blockchain transparency into a tri-level Supply Chain 
Network (SCN) using a bi-objective optimization model. They utilized 
fuzzy Goal Programming (GP) and chance-constrained programming to 
respectively deal with the bi-objectiveness and uncertainty of the model. 
The improved branch and efficiency algorithm assessed transparency, 
cost, and service. Comparing cases emphasized the trade-off between 
blockchain costs and benefits in supply chain design. Meeting customer 
demand is a growing concern for supply chain managers. The 
omni-channel strategy addressed this by integrating online and tradi­
tional channels.
Babaei et al. [28] and Babaei et al. [29] suggested an algorithm for 
assessing omni-channel distribution network configurations using an 
extended Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA) model. The algorithm 
evaluated the network configurations based on cost, service, blockchain 
transparency, and environmental criteria, employing an uncertain 
optimization model for consensus. As economies of scale drive demand 
growth, Babaei et al. [30] proposed a sustainable, customer-centric 
closed-loop SCN design integrating blockchain technology for trans­
parency, as a first-of-its-kind design, applying fuzzy GP in iterative and 
integrated DEA models. Babaei et al. [31] offered a bi-level multi-­
objective DEA-based optimization for production and distribution 
planning, tackling cost and traffic congestion, and incorporating 
blockchain transparency. Solutions, categorized by strategies, empha­
sized either cost or traffic congestion efficiency. In addressing environ­
mental challenges and the dominance of road transportation, Oudani 
[32] explored synchromodal transportation with blockchain technol­
ogy. A multi-objective optimization model integrated transportation and 
blockchain costs for sustainable solutions. With an emphasis on block­
chain’s essential function, especially in sectors dealing with hazardous 
materials, Oudani et al. [33] proposed a green blockchain system. 
Mathematical models optimized energy management for cost-effective 
and eco-friendly blockchain transactions, ensuring security and sus­
tainability. Babaei et al. [34] developed a decision support framework 
using DEA models to evaluate blockchain adoption strategies in the O&G 
supply chain, addressing transparency and efficiency issues. They also 
considered Norway’s O&G sector and represented varying outcomes 
under deterministic and uncertain conditions, offering a comprehensive 
approach to guide strategic decision-making. De Carvalho et al. [35] 
suggested an approach to optimize the adoption of blockchain in a SCN 
configuration. Despite blockchain’s potential to improve transparency, 
efficiency, cost savings, and traceability, its adoption was limited. A 
Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) procedure was utilized by 
Munim et al. [36] for appraising blockchain implementation strategies 
in the O&G industry. Factors like a lack of expertise and collaboration 
significantly impacted the adoption process. The Bayesian Best-Worst 
Method (BWM) identified transformation as the leading strategy for 
accelerating blockchain adoption.
Distributed ledger technologies like blockchain hold the potential for 
enhancing SCM and sustainability, promoting responsible sourcing and 
environmental compliance. Despite its potential, blockchain faces 
limited adoption due to managerial unfamiliarity. Yousefi & Tosarkani 
[37] employed network theory, fuzzy inference system, fuzzy cognitive 
map, and hybrid learning algorithm to find and rank blockchain adop­
tion enablers, demonstrating its remarkable effect on mineral supply

4:
chain performance. Since 2008, academia has increasingly focused on 
blockchain technology, particularly its application in sustainable 
manufacturing. However, technical challenges persist, such as 
consensus algorithms and privacy protection. To address these, a hybrid 
fuzzy MCDM framework was proposed by Su et al. [38], evaluating 
criteria weights and organizational preferences. An empirical case study 
underscores the framework’s effectiveness, highlighting blockchain’s 
potential in transparency, trust establishment, and fraud prevention. 
Yet, further research on blockchain adoption in sustainable 
manufacturing is warranted. Babaei et al. [7] investigated how block­
chain technology could transform SCM by enhancing transparency, 
despite environmental and security challenges. They applied a 
tri-objective optimization model for designing supply chain-blockchain 
technology networks, considering energy consumption, emissions, and 
security.
Table 1 presents a summary of the existing literature on blockchain 
technology and its applications across various supply chain industries. 
This table highlights key studies, their applications, main focus, and 
findings, along with two important conditions that demonstrate sto­
chasticity and calculate risk based on uncertainty and super-efficiency.
2.2. Nexus between blockchain adoption drivers and supply chain 
outcomes
The current body of literature consistently highlights a causal link 
between the drivers of blockchain adoption and improvements in supply 
chain performance. Across various sectors, factors such as technological 
preparedness and the implementation of strategic approaches are 
specified to significantly enhance transparency and operational effec­
tiveness. However, within the O&G industry, this connection remains 
insufficiently examined. Preliminary research, including Munim et al. 
[36], Babaei et al. [39], and Babaei et al. [34], points to organizational, 
technological, and supply chain-related elements, along with stochastic 
influences, as key moderators of blockchain’s effectiveness. Despite 
these insights, there is a notable gap; existing studies have not 
comprehensively assessed how interconnected decision-making criteria, 
under conditions of uncertainty and from the perspectives of 
super-efficiency and risk, shape the success of blockchain strategies in 
real-world O&G supply chain environments. This study addresses that 
gap by introducing a novel NDEA framework designed for such complex 
evaluations.
2.3. Research gaps and contributions
The main research gaps include. 
o Limited research on the evaluation of blockchain strategies in the 
supply chain, particularly in industries like O&G,
o Absence of studies examining the relationships between criteria in 
evaluating blockchain strategies,
o Lack of network evaluation models for assessing blockchain appli­
cation strategies,
o Scarcity of models combining both data-based and optimization- 
based approaches for evaluating blockchain deployment strategies,
o Insufficient consideration of reality-based conditions, such as un­
certainty and discriminating power, in the existing literature on 
blockchain strategy evaluation.
Based on the above-mentioned research gaps, the contributions of 
this work are listed as follows.

I. This work introduces a comprehensive framework for evaluating 
blockchain strategies in the O&G supply chain, addressing the 
limited research in this vital industry and providing key insights 
for performance evaluation and decision-making,
II. It explores the relationships between evaluation criteria in 
blockchain strategies, an area with limited research. It offers in­
sights into their interconnections and provides a deeper under­
standing of blockchain strategy evaluation,
III. It addresses gaps in stochastic risky network evaluation models 
for blockchain strategies by presenting innovative models for 
assessing blockchain networks in supply chains. We introduce a 
hybrid framework that combines data-based and optimization- 
based approaches and incorporates real-world factors for a 
more accurate evaluation of blockchain deployment strategies. 
This approach also derives risk from evaluation results under 
both stochastic and deterministic conditions, which is novel in 
this context. Furthermore, this study utilizes PCA technique 
based on optimal evaluation outcomes. It enables managers to 
benefit from Machine Learning (ML)-driven insights from both 
methodologies and strategies viewpoints.
It should be noted that the investigation of blockchain strategies in 
the O&G sector is very limited. The three notable studies in this area are 
Munim et al. [36], Babaei et al. [39] and Babaei et al. [34]. The main 
novelties here is that the optimization models are developed based on 
linear programming and the focus is on analyzing stochastic conditions 
rather than incorporating very specific uncertainty conditions. Addi­
tionally, the suggested approach calculates risk under both stochastic 
and deterministic conditions and derives this risk from different levels of 
discrimination power in evaluation models, such as traditional and 
super-efficiency models. In addition, the current study extends the 
perspective of Babaei et al. [34] by advancing network data analysis, an 
aspect overlooked in their work.
2.4. Implementation steps of the model
The implementation steps of the model are depicted in Fig. 1 which 
are also described as follows. 
1. Identifying Strategies: Define blockchain deployment strategies, 
focusing on factors like application novelty and coordination, as 
summarized in Table 2,
2. Establishing Evaluation Criteria: Compile criteria categorized 
under supply chain, organization, technology, drivers, and pressures, 
detailed in Table 2,
3. Developing Evaluation Models: Use various NDEA models, incor­
porating real-world conditions such as uncertainty and discrimina­
tion power (Fig. 8),
4. Analyzing Results and Aggregation: Evaluate strategies using 
NDEA, Super-Efficiency Network Data Envelopment Analysis (SEN­
DEA), Uncertain Network Data Envelopment Analysis (UNDEA), and 
Uncertain Super-Efficiency Network DEA (UnSENDEA) models, then 
aggregate results with the help of the super-efficiency CCR model to 
address both risky and non-risky scenarios (Figs. 2–7).
3. Problem statement
In this study, we aim to assess the effectiveness of blockchain

Strategies and critical criteria influencing the blockchain implementation.
Strategy
Description
Single-use
Low novelty and coordination, suitable for small-scale trials.
Localization
High novelty and low coordination, streamlining local networks and enhancing transparency.
Substitution
High coordination, relies on established applications, with caution against potential challenges.
Transformation
High coordination and novelty, signify a significant shift in systems, requiring changes in social, legal, and political 
realms.
Main Criteria
Sub-criteria
Description
Motivators and 
Barriers
Collaboration Pressure
Necessity for collective blockchain implementation, fostering heightened collaboration for optimal technology 
utilization.
Customer Pressure
Customer demand for sustainable products propels blockchain adoption, ensuring credibility and validity within the 
intricate web of the supply chain.
Market Pressure
Market dynamics, especially in sectors like O&G, propel organizations to embrace blockchain for sustainability, 
mitigating emissions, and aligning with evolving expectations.
Drivers
Security Enhancement
Blockchain’s temporal sequencing and consensus protocols amplify information security, circumscribing access to 
digital assets and fortifying secure transactions.
Operational Cost Reduction
Decentralized blockchain systems optimize operations, markedly curtailing costs, particularly in audits and the 
establishment of standardized systems.
Traceability Improvement
Blockchain’s inherent tracking capabilities elevate traceability and transparency, conferring a competitive advantage 
and addressing consumer sustainability considerations.
Organizational 
Barriers
Lack of Expertise in Technology
Scarcity of competencies and comprehension impedes proliferation, constraining the widespread assimilation of 
fundamental blockchain principles.
Lack of Tools for Implementation
Insufficient hardware and software tools lead to challenges, necessitating substantial investments for the effective 
implementation of blockchain technology.
Lack of Benchmarking Data
The dearth of benchmarks and standards impedes technological juxtaposition and evolution within the blockchain 
domain.
Supply Chain 
Barriers
Lack of Customer Awareness
Inadequate customer understanding and suboptimal communication act as impediments to blockchain adoption, 
hindering the dissemination of this transformative technology.
Lack of Partner Collaboration
Partners’ hesitancy to share information hampers the transparent deployment of blockchain technology within SCNs.
Lack of Partner Coordination
Inefficient information sharing and divergent priorities act as constraints, obstructing the efficient adoption of 
blockchain within the supply chain framework.
Technological 
Barriers
Immaturity of the Technology
Blockchain’s nascent stage introduces challenges in scalability, usability, and interoperability, impinging on its 
integration within broader technological ecosystems.
Limited Information Technology 
Infrastructure
Infrastructure constraints, including cost implications, computational capacity, and transaction volume, impact the 
implementation landscape of blockchain technology.
Security Concerns
Despite blockchain’s reputation for security, apprehensions arise from vulnerabilities related to third-party 
involvement and the potential for illicit activities.

structure, and uncertainty considerations. The network structure, 
depicted in Fig. 2, provides the foundation for these models. Subse­
quently, Formulas (6)-(9) facilitate the aggregation of evaluation out­

linear. All the models presented are based on linear programming, 
which ensures the ability to report globally optimal solutions. It is 
important to note that the constraints stem from DEA, which governs the 
relationships between inputs, outputs, and quasi-inputs/outputs.
3.3. Justifications and concepts behind our approach to problem-solving
This study offers a comprehensive approach to assessing the effec­
tiveness of blockchain deployment strategies in the O&G industry. It 
integrates various methodologies and models to address the complex­
ities inherent in assessing such strategies, particularly in the context of 
real-world conditions characterized by uncertainty and diverse perfor­
mance metrics. One of the primary contributions of this work lies in the 
development of a novel decision-aid model, the UnSENDEA model. This 
model not only considers the efficiency of DMUs; i.e., blockchain 
deployment strategies in this case, but also incorporates uncertainty and 
enhances discrimination power in the evaluation process. By intro­
ducing random changes in observed inputs and outputs to address un­
certainty and by removing the observed DMU from the reference set to 
enhance discrimination power, the UnSENDEA model provides a more 
nuanced and realistic assessment of strategy efficiency.
The integration of network structures into the evaluation process is 
another notable aspect of this study. Fig. 2 illustrates how barriers, 
external pressures, and drivers interact within the O&G industry, 
forming a two-stage network model that informs the evaluation frame­
work. This approach recognizes the interconnectedness of factors 
influencing strategy effectiveness and provides a structured method for 
incorporating these dynamics into the evaluation process. Furthermore, 
the study outlines different variations of the evaluation model, including 
the NDEA, SENDEA, and UNDEA models, each offering distinct insights 
into strategy efficiency. By evaluating blockchain deployment strategies 
across these models and consolidating the outcomes using the super- 
efficiency CCR model, the study provides supply chain managers with 
a comprehensive understanding of strategy performance under both 
risky and non-risky scenarios.
The novel decision-aid model proposed in this study addresses a 
critical need in production research by offering a structured and 
network-based framework for designing and managing production sys­
tems and logistics. By employing advanced methodologies such as the 
UnSENDEA model, the research provides an operational tool for 
decision-makers to appraise the effectiveness of blockchain deployment 
strategies. This model not only considers traditional efficiency metrics 
but also incorporates uncertainty and network structures, ensuring a 
more comprehensive assessment of strategy performance. Through the 
systematic integration of evaluation criteria spanning supply chain, 
organizational, technological, and market-driven factors, the decision- 
aid model enables stakeholders to make informed decisions regarding 
strategy selection and resource allocation, ultimately enhancing the ef­
ficiency and resilience of production systems in the O&G industry. 
Moreover, the universality of the decision-aid model makes it accessible 
to a wide audience in production research.
By delineating the evaluation process through the offered method­
ology and illustrative framework, such as Fig. 2 depicting the network 
structure of the O&G industry, the model facilitates understanding and 
application across various organizational contexts. For instance, in this 
study, the O&G industry is examined because it is indeed considered a 
production system. It encompasses stages such as the extraction, 
refinement, and distribution of petroleum and natural gas resources, 
involving complex processes and logistical operations similar to those 
found in manufacturing and production sectors. Additionally, the flex­
ibility of the model, demonstrated through its ability to accommodate 
both risky and non-risky scenarios, ensures its relevance and applica­
bility in dynamic production environments. By integrating various 
models, considering uncertainty, and accounting for network structures, 
the study provides valuable insights that can inform decision-making 
and enhance the management of production systems and logistics in 
the industry. As a result, the decision-aid model presented in this study 
not only contributes to advancing knowledge in production research but 
also offers practical utility for industry practitioners seeking to optimize 
their operations and adapt to evolving market conditions.
It is noteworthy that our article is compared to Munim et al. [36] on 
blockchain strategies in the O&G supply chain, which stands out for its 
innovations. This research delves into evaluation through optimization 
and efficiency, providing decision-makers and managers with more 
precise insights for informed decision-making. Furthermore, we incor­
porate stochastic and risky conditions in decision-making, enhancing 
the accuracy of real-world decision-making processes. As a result, the 
developed approach incorporates linear programming, data optimiza­
tion, and the application of stochastic concepts in optimization models. 
We assume that introducing random variations in inputs and outputs 
effectively captures the uncertainty inherent in real-world conditions. 
Additionally, the study presumes that the different evaluation models 
(NDEA, SENDEA, UNDEA, and UnSENDEA) each provide unique in­
sights into strategy efficiency and are well-suited to capture various 
aspects of performance. This layered modeling approach, as outlined in 
Table 4, ensures a comprehensive and realistic assessment, enabling 
managers to choose strategies based not only on theoretical efficiency 
but also on real-world conditions.
Fig. 3 displays the proposed methodological framework.
4. Results
4.1. Case study
In this section, first, the data used to conduct the study is described. 
Next, the findings and analyses are presented to demonstrate the 
applicability and validity of the proposed methodology. Data3 were 
sourced from Munim et al. [36] and obtained through a web survey 
distributed across diverse departments in four key Norwegian O&G 
companies: Aker BP, Equinor, BW Offshore, GE O&G Offshore, 
Schlumberger Limited, and DNV. The data are displayed in Appendix A.

13:
automation, heightened security, and considerable cost efficiencies 
[46]. In response to growing shareholder demands for cost efficiency 
and higher profits, the O&G sector is increasingly turning to blockchain 
to streamline its supply chains through enhanced transparency and 
automation.
Chevron Technology Ventures (CTV) was created in 1999 in order to 
incorporate external technologies aimed at enhancing Chevron’s energy 
production and distribution, with a strategic emphasis on affordability, 
reliability, and environmental sustainability. Recently, Chevron part­
nered with Carbon Management Solutions (CMS) and Xpansiv on a 
blockchain pilot in Singapore in order to improve the traceability and 
auditability of lower-carbon fuel oil transactions via an asset ledger. 
CMS serves as a decarbonization platform within the commodities 
market, whereas Xpansiv offers the underlying infrastructure for the 
management and exchange of environmental commodities [47]. Simi­
larly, Repsol [54], a leading Spanish energy company, collaborated with 
Finboot and its Technology Lab to employ blockchain technology for 
improving product certification. This partnership led to the creation of 
BlockLabs, a platform that digitizes sample approval processes and re­
duces inefficiencies like rework and communication delays (Repsol, Jan 
29, 2024).
Beyond its origins in digital finance, blockchain demonstrates 
considerable potential across diverse domains, particularly in industrial 
SCM. Traceability plays an important role for companies seeking 
enhanced trust, the appreciation of sustainability initiatives, and effec­
tive communication with customers. Launched in 2018, The Energy 
Origin (TEO) employs blockchain technology, providing a blockchain- 
based solution to trace the origin of energy. The platform enables the 
creation of an auditable trail for certifying the origin and distinctive 
attributes of energy production [48]. In the emerging landscape of the 
grand transition to lower-carbon energy sources, there are broader im­
plications. According to Forbes [49], it is anticipated that numerous 
industrial companies will adopt blockchain networks for the monitoring 
and tabulation of their carbon emissions. Pemex, a major global petro­
leum producer, has unveiled plans to integrate a blockchain technology 
platform for O&G SCM [50]. Joining the Petrobloq4 Global Blockchain 
Industry Consortium, operated by Petroteq Energy Inc., marks a stra­
tegic move for Pemex. As a pioneer in accepting cryptocurrency pay­
ments, Pemex aims to optimize its extensive operations through 
blockchain adoption, enhancing efficiency from production to sales.
5.2. Model development
Considering blockchain’s growing significance in the O&G supply 
chain, there is a notable gap in research on evaluating its application 
strategies within this industry. This study utilizes data-driven and 
optimization-based models for blockchain deployment strategies, 
considering innovativeness and complexity. Criteria encompass supply 
chain, organization, technology, and pressures. The developed evalua­
tion models incorporate factors of uncertainty and discrimination that 
are crucial for supply chain decision-making. Our exploration of 
blockchain strategies in the Norwegian O&G sector, as depicted in 
Table 5, exhibits diverse outcomes based on super-efficiency and un­
certainty conditions. Fig. 4 shows the superior discrimination power of 
super-efficient models in evaluating blockchain strategies. Fig. 5 reveals 
different robust capacities among the models, with super-efficient 
models demonstrating satisfactory robustness in ranking blockchain 
deployment strategies. In Fig. 6, despite the single-use strategy securing 
high scores across evaluation methods, it represents significant score 
dispersion. In summary, our study is delineated into two phases as 
illustrated in Fig. 14.
As can be seen in Fig. 14, the first phase entails evaluating 
approaches through various data-driven models. Subsequently, in the 
second phase, the evaluation findings are synthesized under risky and 
non-risky conditions, empowering supply chain managers to make 
informed and comprehensive decisions.
5.3. Insights and implications
Our approach, findings, and discussions provide critical insights into 
practical applications in production systems and logistics, with a focus 
on the O&G sector. The research integrates data-driven models and 
optimization strategies into a framework that is computationally effi­
cient, making it well-suited for practical use in production and logistics 
environments. The quick solution times of less than 0.1 s achieved with 
GAMS software emphasize the practicality of applying these models in 
real-time operations. The evaluation results presented in Table 5
demonstrate the effectiveness of various strategies, including single-use, 
substitution, and localization, for optimizing production and logistics 
operations. These findings help decision-makers improve resource 
allocation and operational efficiency. On the other hand, this work 
tackles the challenge of uncertainty in decision-making, which is 
indisputable for dynamic production and logistics settings. By incorpo­
rating uncertainty factors and utilizing models like UnSENDEA, 
decision-makers can more accurately assess and manage risks, thus 
enhancing resilience and adaptability in their supply chains.
5.3.1. General managerial insights
The use of blockchain technology in the O&G supply chain, exem­
plified by Chevron’s pilot initiative in Singapore and Repsol’s partner­
ship with Finboot, demonstrates the real-world applicability of our 
approach. Blockchain technology provides significant benefits in terms 
of improved transparency, traceability and operational efficiency, key 
components of contemporary production systems, and logistics man­
agement. The most important general managerial insights are given as 
follows. 
i. Strategy Effectiveness: It is necessary to assess the performance 
of various approaches, including single-use, substitution, and 
localization, and identify the most effective methods for 
enhancing production and logistics operations,
ii. Uncertainty: Managers must acknowledge how uncertainty af­
fects efficiency scores and focus on strategies that provide resil­
ience and stability in unpredictable conditions,
iii. Blockchain Solutions: It is helpful to investigate how blockchain 
technology can be integrated into the supply chain to enhance 
transparency, traceability, and operational efficiency,
iv. Resource Allocation: Policymakers must implement evaluation 
models to strategically allocate resources, maximizing produc­
tion and logistics effectiveness,
v. Robust Evaluation Methods: It is necessary to employ evalua­
tion techniques to guarantee reliable and accurate decision- 
making.
5.3.2. General perspectives
On the other hand, our framework offers numerous valuable per­
spectives, as outlined below. 
⁃ Integration of Emerging Technologies: Our framework presents an op­
portunity to explore the integration of emerging technologies, such 
as advanced Artificial Intelligence (AI) and other emerging tech­
nologies, to enhance real-time efficiency measurement in production 
systems and logistics within the O&G industry,
⁃ Dynamic Decision Support Systems: There is scope for creating decision 
support systems that dynamically respond to temporal variations in 
environmental conditions and market dynamics,

to address the intricate supply chain requirements of the Oil & Gas sector.

6.2. Policy suggestions
This work provided a strong evidence supporting the strategic use of 
blockchain technology in the O&G sector, highlighting the importance 
of data-driven decisions and evaluation frameworks. First, prioritizing 
the creation of a comprehensive blockchain strategy at the national or 
industry level is essential. This strategy should align with specific re­
quirements of the O&G sector, goals of SCM, technological integration, 
and organizational aspects. According to the findings, strategies like 
single-use and substitution achieve the highest operational efficiency, 
making them suitable for early-stage adoption. Risk management and 
decision-support systems can integrate advanced analytical models like 
the UnSENDEA framework proposed in the study. These models, which 
include super-efficiency and uncertainty considerations, provide a 
robust basis for evaluating blockchain strategies. The outcomes further 
encourage collaboration across industries and the integration of block­
chain frameworks into renewable energy and smart grid infrastructures. 
This development mirrors global priorities in sustainability, alongside 
the intensified emphasis on tracking and responsibility. In other words, 
using the same assessment frameworks from the O&G industry in 
renewable energy supply chains helps ensure methodological consis­
tency and encourages innovative solutions. On the other hand, the 
generalizability of our proposed models makes them adaptable to 
diverse contexts, which supports broader applications in the energy 
sector. In this regard, industry-level benchmarks for efficiency must be 
developed to enable meaningful comparisons of blockchain adoption 
among organizations. The findings on confidence levels (95% vs. 99%) 
emphasize the importance of flexible decision-making frameworks that 
effectively strike a balance between efficiency with uncertainty.
6.3. Future directions
Future research may focus on developing additional DEA models, 
applying our evaluation models in O&G companies, and adapting them 
for application in other industries. Doing so enables researchers to assess 
the models’ real-world effectiveness and gain meaningful understanding 
of their practical use and impact. Given the relevance of blockchain 
technology across different sectors, future studies could explore the 
adaptation of the evaluation models for application in other industries 
facing similar challenges and opportunities. This analysis across in­
dustries could uncover the extent to which blockchain adoption strate­
gies are transferable and the implications for sectors such as 
manufacturing, logistics, and healthcare. For example, the application of 
the developed UnSENDEA and SENDEA models can be taken into ac­
count across various geographical regions through collaboration with 
reputable O&G companies. This would allow researchers and practi­
tioners to validate the effectiveness of these models under varying reg­
ulatory, environmental, and operational conditions. In particular, it 
would provide an opportunity to assess how well the single-use and 
substitution strategies, identified as top performers in this study, pro­
duce significant efficiency improvements when adopted widely. On the 
other hand, future research could benefit from applying fuzzy logic [51,
52] and robust optimization [53] to model uncertainties in data and 
address the inherent variability. This form of uncertainty allows for a 
more efficient treatment of qualitative data; e.g., expert evaluations or 
stakeholder opinions, thereby improving the models’ adaptability.
